data.title: Hercules Capital expects robust originations in 2026 amid platform expansion and defensive positioning
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hercules Capital (HTGC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Scott Bluestein highlighted, “2025 was another year of record operating performance, record originations, platform expansion and strong and stable credit for Hercules Capital.” He noted new annual records for total and net investment income, originations of nearly $4 billion, and gross fundings of $2.28 billion.
  • Bluestein emphasized, “Despite operating in a declining rate environment, we were able to achieve 120% coverage of our quarterly base distribution of $0.40 per share in the fourth quarter and maintain $0.82 per share of spillover income.” He confirmed no change to the quarterly base distribution and continuation of the same supplemental distribution as last year.
  • Hercules Capital’s assets under management surpassed $5.7 billion, marking a more than 20% increase from year-end 2024. Bluestein stated, “Record new debt and equity commitments of $3.92 billion, an increase of 45.7% year-over-year... Record net debt portfolio growth of approximately $748.5 million.”
  • The CEO pointed to a defensive approach with a focus on asset diversification and maintaining “higher-than-normal first lien exposure, which was approximately 90% again in Q4.”
  • On business mix, Bluestein said, “In Q4, approximately 69% of our commitments and about half of our fundings were to life sciences companies, while approximately 31% of our new commitments were to tech companies.”
  • CFO Seth Meyer stated, “2025 was another very strong year for Hercules Capital with record operating performance and an acceleration of the growth of the Hercules platform.” He highlighted, “Total investment income in Q4 was $137.4 million, supported by our year-to-date debt portfolio growth. Core investment income, a non-GAAP measure, increased as well to a record $133.3 million.”

Outlook

  • Bluestein projected, “With the expansion of our platform capabilities over the last several years, and our expectation for continued market volatility, we expect a very robust new business environment for Hercules in 2026.”
  • He described expectations for “more strategic M&A, more capital markets activity, and more support for the innovation economy in 2026.”
  • Meyer provided guidance: “For the first quarter, we expect our core yield to be in the middle of our previous disclosed range of 12% to 12.5%... we expect $150 million to $200 million in prepayment activity in the first quarter.”
  • The company expects SG&A expenses of $26 million to $27 million and an RIA expense allocation of approximately $4.5 million for Q1 2026.

Financial Results

  • Hercules Capital reported Q4 total investment income of $137.4 million and net investment income of $87 million, or $0.48 per share.
  • The company generated a return on equity in Q4 of 16.4% and a GAAP effective yield of 12.9% (impacted by lower early payoffs), with a core yield of 12.5%.
  • GAAP leverage increased to 104.4% in Q4, up from 99.5% in Q3, remaining at the low end of the typical historical range.
  • Net asset value per share in Q4 was $12.13, an increase of 0.7% from Q3.
  • Q4 net realized gains totaled $20.3 million. Liquidity stood at $525.5 million in the BDC and over $1 billion across the platform, further enhanced by a $300 million bond offering post quarter-end.

Q&A

  • Brian McKenna, Citizens JMP Securities: Asked about opportunities amid volatility and AI. Bluestein responded, “We absolutely think that there is an interesting opportunity here for us to play offense... Our Q1 quarter-to-date numbers are as strong as we've ever announced on a Q4 call.”
  • McKenna questioned RIA fundraising. Bluestein replied, “We absolutely expect to continue to raise additional capital throughout 2026. We expect Fund IV to have a final close in 2026 and discussions are already well underway for what will be the next vehicle.”
  • Crispin Love, Piper Sandler: Inquired about software portfolio resilience. Bluestein indicated confidence, noting the company focuses on “software companies that are focused on providing core mission-critical solutions... integrating AI agentic solutions.”
  • Love also asked about M&A and IPO activity. Bluestein expects robust M&A in 2026, especially as “acquisitions from larger competitors... are picking up some smaller competitors that are distressed from a valuation perspective,” but anticipates the IPO market to “remain muted.”
  • Finian O'Shea, Wells Fargo: Questioned dividend sustainability and growth. Bluestein responded, “We have tremendous conviction in the growth opportunity for the platform this year... our pipeline is not showing any signs of slowing down.”
  • Douglas Harter, UBS: Asked about balancing opportunity and patience. Bluestein said, “We're being aggressive, we're picking our spots, but I would describe it as targeted.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, with detailed questions about the sustainability of originations, dividend policy, and the impact of AI on the software portfolio.
  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, with Bluestein stating, “We believe that we are incredibly well positioned to benefit from a more favorable originations market in 2026.”
  • Compared to the prior quarter, management’s tone remains confident, particularly about platform resilience and the ability to capitalize on market volatility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 demonstrated continued record originations, but net investment income per share declined slightly from $0.49 to $0.48.
  • The company’s base distribution and supplemental distributions remain unchanged, signaling stable payout policy.
  • The life sciences share of commitments increased to 69% from 54% in Q3, reflecting a more defensive posture.
  • Credit quality improved, with a lower percentage of loans in higher-risk grades and only one loan on nonaccrual at quarter-end, down from two in Q3.
  • Management’s confidence and focus on disciplined underwriting persisted, while analyst questions increasingly focused on future growth drivers and risk mitigation amid market volatility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Bluestein cautioned about “higher-than-normal market and macro volatility” and observed “the recent valuation reset that is taking place in certain parts of the tech ecosystem.”
  • He noted a “slightly more defensive positioning of the portfolio,” especially with increased life sciences exposure.
  • Meyer flagged expectations for increased interest expense due to debt portfolio growth and anticipated a slight decline in core yield in Q1 2026 following a Fed rate cut.

Final Takeaway

Hercules Capital delivered another quarter of record performance, marked by strong originations, portfolio growth, and disciplined risk management. Management maintains a confident outlook for 2026, expecting robust new business activity and continued resilience amid market volatility, while keeping a defensive stance through portfolio diversification and prudent underwriting standards.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Global shipping industry sticks with green investments, despite carbon price delay - report
data.description:
The shipping industry’s biggest players are said to be shrugging off the Trump administration's opposition to a global carbon price and are forging ahead with billions of dollars in emissions-reducing investments, according to company officials and a Reuters analysis of data. 

Europe, Brazil, and a host of other nations are pushing the sector, which is responsible for nearly 3% of the world’s greenhouse gas emissions, to go green, the report said.

Some analysts and industry observers initially warned that the absence of such a global framework added complexity to companies’ planning and could cause some to pause their green investments. 

But in interviews with 15 shipping companies, ports, bunker suppliers, and marine technology companies, 10 told Reuters that regional regulations, long investment lead times, and expectations of a continuing trend towards decarbonization all argued in favour of staying the course. And a Reuters analysis of vessel delivery data through 2028 showed orders of ships capable of running on alternative fuels dominating new shipbuilding.

While Pacific Basin (PCFBF) - a large dry bulk ship owner - did opt to buy four newbuild vessels running only on oil-derived fuels, citing the postponement of the IMO’s carbon price, the company is an outlier, the report added.

By the end of December, companies had invested over $150 billion in dual-fuel vessels, according to a Reuters analysis of data from the World Shipping Council, an industry group for container shipping and vehicle carriers.

Maersk (AMKBY), among the first to explore emissions-reducing alternative fuels, initially opted for methanol but has since ordered LNG-fueled ships and has now also started testing ethanol as an alternative.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:BOAT), (NYSEARCA:BWET)

Relevant companies include Frontline (NYSE:FRO), Teekay (TK), Tsakos Energy Navigation (TEN), and CMB. Tech (CMBT), Navios Maritime Partners (NMM), Scorpio Tankers (STNG), DHT Holdings (DHT), International Seaways (INSW), SFL Corp. (SFL), and Maersk (OTCPK:AMKBY) (OTCPK:AMKAF).

data.title: Asian markets retreat from records as ‘AI scare’ triggers global tech rout; Taiwan inks historic 15% tariff cap with U.S.
data.description:
Equities in Asia fell Friday, following a tech-driven retreat in U.S. markets. Amid the red tape, a significant trade agreement was finalized between Washington and Taipei, lowering Taiwanese export tariffs to 15%. The new rate brings Taiwan in line with Japan and South Korea.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) fell to around $66,000 on Friday, giving up most of its recent gains as cryptocurrencies came under renewed pressure.

Gold rose to around $4,960 per ounce on Friday after falling more than 3% in the previous session, as markets navigated elevated volatility.

Malaysia’s economy expanded 6.3% year-on-year in Q4 2025, revised up from an initial 5.7% and accelerating from 5.4% growth in Q3.

The benchmark KOSPI rose 0.36% to around 5,545 on Friday, building on record-high levels. The South Korean won fell to around 1,442 per dollar, easing after reaching a two-week high in the previous session.

Japan (NKY:IND) fell 1.28% to below 57,000, while the broader Topix declined 0.7% to 3,855 on Friday, retreating from record highs. The Japanese yen slipped past 153 per dollar on Friday but remained on track for its largest weekly gain since November 2024.

China (SHCOMP) fell 0.72% to around 4,120, while the Shenzhen Component declined 0.6% to 14,200 on Friday, reversing gains from the previous session, and the offshore yuan slipped to around 6.90 per dollar on Friday, snapping a sixth straight day of gains and retreating from a 33-month high reached in the previous session.

Meanwhile, fresh data showed China’s new home prices slid 3.3% yoy in January, the steepest drop in seven months, underscoring Beijing’s struggle to stabilize property markets and further pressuring sentiment. 

On the geopolitical front, the US has reportedly paused several major tech-security measures targeting China ahead of a planned April meeting between Presidents Donald Trump and Xi Jinping.

Trading will pause Monday through Thursday for the Lunar New Year holiday.

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 2.14% to 26,588 in early Friday trade, extending losses for a second session.

India (SENSEX) fell 0.93% to 82,878, declining for the third straight session and hitting its lowest level since February 2. The Indian rupee slipped to around 90.6 per dollar, retreating from recent gains as a broad risk-off sentiment and weakness across Asian currencies weighed on the market. 

Australia (AS51) fell 1.39%, sliding more than 1% to around 8,940 in Friday morning trade, snapping a two-session rally. 

In the U.S. on Thursday, all three major indexes ended in the red as early gains evaporated amid extended pressure from the tech sector.

The stronger-than-expected jobs report earlier in the week continued to push back expectations for near-term Federal Reserve rate cuts ahead of Friday’s CPI release.

U.S. stock futures steadied on Friday after the major averages sold off in the previous session on renewed AI concerns, while investors awaited the January consumer price index report: Dow -0.20%; S&P 500 -0.17%; Nasdaq -0.23%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia's markets and economy:

data.title: Norsk Hydro Non-GAAP EPS of NOK 0.70, revenue of NOK 47.22B
data.description:
• Norsk Hydro press release (NHYDY): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of NOK 0.70.
  • Revenue of NOK 47.22B (-14.2% Y/Y).
  • Hydro’s adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter of 2025 was NOK 5,587 million, down from NOK 7,701 million in the same quarter last year.
  • Adjusted net debt decreased from NOK 21.1 billion to NOK 18.2 billion.
  • The capital expenditure guidance for the full year was reduced from NOK 15 billion to NOK 13.5 billion in the second quarter. The actual cash effective spend in 2025 ended at NOK 12.1 billion, strengthening the financial flexibility in the face of market unrest and uncertainty.

data.title: Coal miner China Shenhua gets approval for $19B asset acquisition plan
data.description:
• Chinese regulators have cleared a proposal for the nation’s largest coal producer to purchase 133.6 billion yuan ($19 billion) in assets from its parent company, a deal designed to support the miner as coal demand begins to level off.
  • China Shenhua Energy (CSUAY) (CUAEF) will take stakes in 12 units of China Energy Investment Corp., including coal-to-chemicals, mining, power generation, and logistics, it said in an exchange filing on Thursday.
  • The move would deepen its vertical integration and expand the total value of its assets to nearly 900 billion yuan, it said.

data.title: Sylvamo outlines $300M+ free cash flow potential as Eastover investments drive 2026 transition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sylvamo Corporation (SLVM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO John Sims began by sharing his vision for Sylvamo to become “legendary” through world-class excellence in safety, employee engagement, customer centricity, operational excellence, cost leadership, and sustainability.
  • Sims stated, “We will set a new standard for customer experience and loyalty, striving to be truly outstanding. Our commitment is to deliver superior value and service to our customers, earning their trust and loyalty. This is critical to our strategy.”
  • He highlighted the discontinuation of quarterly adjusted EBITDA outlooks, explaining, “We believe this change further aligns our external communications with how we manage the business and our goal to attract and retain high-quality long-term shareowners who share our vision of long-term value creation.”
  • The company reported a 12% return on invested capital for 2025, $448 million in adjusted EBITDA, $44 million in free cash flow, and $155 million cash returned to shareholders. Reinvestment of $224 million focused on manufacturing network and Brazil forestlands.
  • Sims described a 9% quarter-over-quarter increase in uncoated freesheet sales volume and improved productivity in paper machines.
  • CFO Donald Devlin noted, “In the fourth quarter, we earned $125 million of adjusted EBITDA compared to $151 million in the prior quarter. Price and mix was unfavorable by $21 million, primarily due to mix across the regions as well as lower paper prices in Europe and some of our Brazilian export markets. Volume increased by $18 million, largely due to Latin America and North America.”

Outlook

  • The company has discontinued providing quarterly and full-year adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow guidance to emphasize long-term capital allocation and value creation.
  • Capital spending outlook for 2026 is $245 million, primarily for the Eastover mill investment. This is expected to normalize in 2027 as projects wind down.
  • Sylvamo expects North America adjusted EBITDA impacts to total approximately $65 million in 2026, with an additional $10 million charge in Q1 from International Paper due to Riverdale mill energy costs. Europe faces a $20 million adjusted EBITDA impact from tariffs and freight on U.S. shipments.
  • Management anticipates 2026 to be a transition year, with $85 million in one-time costs largely not repeating in 2027 and aims for future annual free cash flow greater than $300 million and returns on invested capital above 15%.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA was $125 million with a margin of 14% and free cash flow of $38 million. Adjusted operating earnings were $1.08 per share.
  • Full-year adjusted EBITDA was $448 million with a 13% margin, free cash flow of $44 million, and adjusted operating earnings of $3.54 per share.
  • Compared to Q3, Q4 saw reduced EBITDA and margin, driven by unfavorable price/mix, higher planned maintenance costs, and input/transportation costs.
  • The company highlighted a $45 million negative adjusted EBITDA impact in North America for 2026 due to sourcing mix, conversion, freight, and outage costs, as well as a $25 million working capital impact related to inventory build and drawdown.

Q&A

  • Daniel Harriman, Sidoti & Company: Asked about improving mix and winning new customers in Europe, and margin improvement drivers. CEO Sims explained the Saillat mill investment allows more roll business and that order books are full. He stated, “Margins are very compressed. We've been significantly working to reduce costs at all our facilities... However, we do need the market to improve, and we're seeing that.”
  • George Staphos, BofA Securities: Inquired about investor questions on capital allocation and the long-term fit of Nymolla. Sims responded there has been alignment with investors: “We're not changing our strategy. We're going to be focused on uncoated freesheet nor will we be changing our capital allocation strategy.” He said wood costs at Nymolla are finally starting to decrease, and all options are being considered to improve European performance.
  • Matthew McKellar, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about the impact of Lenzing’s TreeToTextile facility on fiber costs (CFO Devlin said there is none), and about the scope of lean management and digital transformation. Sims stated, “We're raising the bar in terms of our improvement initiatives, and we believe that the lean principles, the lean will be a key driver of that.”
  • McKellar also questioned the pause in share repurchases. Devlin replied, “As we look into 2026, the plans we have, the capital intensity plus the inventory build... we thought it was prudent not to make share repurchases in the quarter.”
  • Staphos highlighted the $10 million Riverdale charge and asked about dropping guidance. Devlin confirmed the $10 million is in addition to the $85 million transition costs. Sims explained, “We manage the business on a long-term basis. That's how we focus on, not on a quarterly basis.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on European pricing, cost structures, transition impacts, and guidance transparency, with a slightly negative tone on persistent European margin compression and the discontinuation of guidance.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing transformation and long-term value, but was more defensive during Q&A, particularly regarding guidance: “We believe that we don't want to -- we manage the business on a long-term basis.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, there was a more defensive posture from management and increased skepticism from analysts about the lack of guidance and challenges in Europe.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted with the official discontinuation of quarterly and annual EBITDA and free cash flow targets, while the long-term strategic direction remains focused on high-return investments and operational excellence.
  • Management underscored the 2026 transitional impacts, which were discussed previously but provided greater detail on the specific EBITDA and cash flow impacts this quarter.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from working capital and inventory build in Q3 to the sustainability of European operations, transition execution, and capital allocation rationale in Q4.
  • Sentiment moved from cautious optimism to increased analyst skepticism and more explicit management defensiveness, especially regarding guidance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing challenges in the European market, particularly margin compression due to low prices and high wood costs, though some improvement is anticipated with declining wood costs and announced price increases.
  • North America faces significant one-time costs and reduced volume during the transition, with a $95 million negative EBITDA impact in 2026 related to sourcing, outages, and energy costs.
  • Analysts expressed concern about the company’s ability to offset European headwinds and manage through the North American transition, as well as the decision to discontinue detailed guidance.

Final Takeaway

Sylvamo’s leadership reaffirmed its commitment to long-term value creation and capital discipline, highlighting 2026 as a pivotal transition year marked by strategic investments at the Eastover mill and significant one-time costs that are not expected to repeat. Management emphasized operational transformation, lean management, and customer-centricity as key drivers for future earnings growth and free cash flow exceeding $300 million annually, despite current European headwinds and short-term margin pressures. The company’s focus remains on strengthening its competitive position and delivering sustained shareholder returns as transition-related investments reach completion and market conditions gradually improve.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. Senate stalls Homeland Security funds, raising chances of another government shutdown
data.description:
The Senate Democrats blocked legislation that would advance the bill to fund the Department of Homeland Security ((DHS)) on Thursday, which could possibly lead to another partial government shutdown.

The 52-47 vote failed to reach the 60-vote threshold needed to advance funds. The funding for DHS, which oversees Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Customs and Border Protection, is set to lapse at 12 a.m. ET Saturday. 

The funding would have allocated $64.4 billion through September 30 to fund immigration enforcement and other responsibilities under the Department of Homeland Security, including airport security, disaster response, the U.S. Coast Guard, and the U.S. Secret Service.

“Democrats have been very clear: We will not support an extension of the status quo,” Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer said on the Senate floor ahead of the vote.

"Two weeks ago we agreed to extend funding while we talked and tried to find a pathway forward. However, the timeline we knew was going to be short," Sen. Katie Britt, an Alabama Republican, told CBS News. "We are working in good faith to find a pathway forward. What we're asking is, let us continue to do that."

The bill’s approval may have had only a limited practical impact.  Last fall, DHS classified 258,000 of its 272,000 employees as “essential” and required them to continue working during the record 43-day shutdown that ended on November 13.

In addition, DHS’s two primary immigration enforcement agencies—Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Customs and Border Protection—received substantial budget increases from Congress last year, separate from the funding that is currently stalled in Congress.

A DHS shutdown would furlough nonessential workers across agencies like TSA, Coast Guard, FEMA, and Secret Service, though critical operations would continue. A shutdown of “nonessential” operations is imminent if Congress does not act.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
